title: How to work out your own EV charging cost
description: Work out exactly what charging your EV costs with one simple formula, plus real pence-per-mile figures for home, off-peak and public charging in 2026.
blurb: One formula turns your unit rate and your car's efficiency into a true pence-per-mile charging cost.
lede: Ask ten EV drivers what a charge costs them and you will get ten guesses, because the answer hides behind three numbers most people never multiply together: the size of the charge in kilowatt hours, the price paid per kilowatt hour, and the slice of energy lost on the way into the battery. Pull those three out and the sum takes thirty seconds, works for any car on any tariff at any charger, and tells you precisely whether that overnight tariff, that lamp-post charger or that motorway rapid is good value. This guide walks through the calculation with current UK rates, then shows you how to replace estimates with measured figures from your own car.
short_answer: Multiply the kWh added by your price per kWh, then add about 10% for charging losses if you charged at home. A typical 60 kWh EV costs about £17 to fill on the standard 26.11p rate (Ofgem cap, July to September 2026), £4 to £5 on an EV tariff's off-peak window, and around £47 on a 79p public rapid charger. Per mile, that is roughly 8p, 2p and 23p respectively for a car doing 3.5 miles per kWh.

sections.html:
Every charging cost calculation, whether on a driveway in Doncaster or a motorway rapid charger, comes down to the same sum:

  • kWh added × price per kWh = cost of the charge

A kilowatt hour is simply the unit your electricity meter counts in, the same one your kettle and washing machine use. If the idea is fuzzy, our guide to what a kWh actually is covers it in two minutes. For EV charging you need two numbers: how many kWh went into the battery, and what you paid for each one.

There are three ways to find the kWh figure, in descending order of accuracy:

  • Your charger or charging app. Home wallboxes and nearly all public chargers report the energy delivered per session. This is the gold standard because it includes everything you were billed for.
  • Your car's trip computer. Most EVs log energy used per trip or per 100 miles. This measures energy leaving the battery, so it slightly understates what you paid (more on losses below).
  • Battery percentage maths. Usable capacity × the percentage you added. Charging a 60 kWh car from 20% to 80% adds 0.6 × 60 = 36 kWh into the battery.

The price per kWh is on your bill, your smart meter in-home display, or your tariff page in your supplier's app. On a single-rate tariff at the current price cap it is 26.11p. On an EV tariff you will have two rates, and only the off-peak one matters if you schedule your charging into the cheap window.

sections.h2: Turn it into a pence-per-mile figure
sections.html:
Cost per charge is useful, but cost per mile is the number that lets you compare an EV with a petrol car, or home charging with public charging. The sum is:

  • Pence per mile = unit rate (p/kWh) ÷ efficiency (miles per kWh)

Your car's efficiency is the EV equivalent of mpg, and it is displayed on the dashboard or in the trip menu. Typical real-world figures run from about 2.5 miles per kWh for a heavy SUV to 4.5 for a small, slippery hatchback, with 3.5 a fair middle estimate. Our guide to miles per kWh lists what to expect by vehicle type.

Worked example at the price cap rate of 26.11p per kWh and 3.5 miles per kWh:

  • 26.11 ÷ 3.5 = 7.5p per mile before charging losses, about 8.2p once you include them.

The same car on a 7p off-peak rate costs 2p per mile, and on a 79p rapid charger about 22.6p per mile. For reference, a 45 mpg petrol car at 159p a litre costs about 16p per mile. That is the whole economics of EV ownership in one line: where you charge moves the cost by a factor of ten.

Not every kWh that passes through your meter ends up in the battery. Some energy is lost as heat in the onboard charger, the cable and the battery itself, and your meter bills you for all of it. Ignore this and your calculated cost will sit 10 to 15% below reality.

  • Home wallbox (7 kW AC): expect roughly 10% losses. A 60 kWh battery filled from empty draws about 66 kWh through the meter.
  • Three-pin granny lead (2.3 kW): losses rise to roughly 15%, because the car's electronics run for far longer per kWh delivered. The unit rate is identical, so a three-pin charge costs a little more per mile, not less. See wallbox versus three-pin plug for the full comparison.
  • Public rapid (DC): you are billed for energy measured at the charger, and DC charging skips the car's onboard converter, so the billed figure is close to what the battery receives. No adjustment needed.

The practical rule: if you take the kWh figure from the car's own trip computer, add about 10% before multiplying by your unit rate. If you take it from the charger or your smart meter, losses are already included.

sections.h2: What charging actually costs: the numbers
sections.html:
Here is the formula applied to a typical 60 kWh EV doing 3.5 miles per kWh, including 10% losses on home AC charging. The full-charge column assumes filling the whole usable battery; in practice most people top up 30 to 60% at a time.

Where you chargeRate paidFull charge (60 kWh)Cost per mile
Home, standard tariff26.11p/kWh£17.238.2p
Home, EV off-peak window7p/kWh£4.622.2p
Home, cheapest smart tariff5.5p/kWh£3.631.7p
Public standard (lamp post, car park)54p/kWh£32.4015.4p
Public rapid (motorway)79p/kWh£47.4022.6p
Petrol comparison, 45 mpg159p/litren/a16.1p

Home rates: 26.11p per kWh is the Ofgem price cap for July to September 2026; off-peak examples reflect typical EV tariff windows of 5.5p to 9p in mid 2026. Public rates are Zapmap mid-2026 averages. Petrol at 159p per litre, mid 2026. Home figures include 10% charging losses.

Over a year of 8,000 miles, those per-mile figures become roughly £655 on the standard rate, £175 on a 7p off-peak rate, and about £1,800 if you relied entirely on rapid chargers. The petrol car covering the same miles would burn through about £1,285. The full annual breakdown is in our guide to EV running costs versus petrol.

sections.h2: Measure it rather than estimate it
sections.html:
Estimates are fine for a first pass, but your own measured numbers are better, and getting them takes almost no effort:

  • Wallbox app. Most smart chargers log every session with kWh and, if you have entered your tariff, the cost. One month of data gives you a true average that includes losses, pre-heating and everything else.
  • Smart meter. Compare overnight consumption on charging nights against non-charging nights. The difference is your car, measured at the point you pay for it.
  • Plug-in energy monitor. If you charge from a three-pin socket, a £10 to £15 monitor between the socket and the granny lead records exactly what each charge draws. Our guide to plug-in energy monitors explains what to buy and how to read the results.
  • Car's lifetime efficiency screen. Note the miles per kWh figure once a month. Multiply your unit rate by 1.1, divide by that figure, and you have a current, honest pence-per-mile number.

One subtlety worth checking: if you are on an EV tariff, confirm your charger is actually scheduling into the cheap window. A charger that starts the moment you plug in at 6pm pays peak rates for the first several hours. Most tariff apps show a session-by-session rate breakdown, and our guide to the best time to charge an electric car covers how to set the schedule properly.

Your EV charging cost is kWh added multiplied by your unit rate, plus about 10% for losses if you charge at home on AC. For a typical car that means roughly 8p per mile on a standard tariff, 2p on an off-peak EV tariff and over 20p on public rapids. The single most valuable thing you can do with this maths is run it for your own car and your own rates, because the answer almost always points the same way: get as much of your charging as possible onto a cheap overnight rate at home. Our hub on home EV charging costs takes the next step from there.

faq.q: How many kWh does it take to fully charge an electric car?
faq.a: Roughly the battery's usable capacity plus losses. A car with a 60 kWh usable battery draws about 66 kWh through a home meter for a full charge, because around 10% is lost as heat during AC charging. In practice most drivers top up 30 to 60% at a time rather than charging from empty, so a typical session draws 20 to 40 kWh.

faq.q: Should I include charging losses in my calculation?
faq.a: Yes, if your kWh figure comes from the car rather than the charger. The car reports energy into the battery, but your meter bills for energy into the car, which is about 10% higher on a wallbox and up to 15% higher on a three-pin plug. If you take the figure from your charger app or smart meter, losses are already counted.

faq.q: How do I find my electricity unit rate?
faq.a: It is on any recent bill, in your supplier's app under tariff details, or on your smart meter in-home display. On the July to September 2026 price cap it averages 26.11p per kWh. If you are on an EV or time-of-use tariff you will have two or more rates, so check both the off-peak rate and the hours it applies.

faq.q: Why does my EV cost more per mile to charge in winter?
faq.a: Cold batteries accept charge less efficiently and the car spends energy on cabin and battery heating, so efficiency can drop from 3.5 miles per kWh to under 3. The price per kWh does not change, but each mile needs more of them, typically adding 15 to 30% to winter charging costs.

faq.q: Is it cheaper to charge little and often or in one big session?
faq.a: At home the cost is almost identical either way, since you pay per kWh regardless of session size. The exception is an off-peak tariff with a fixed cheap window: smaller, more frequent charges are easier to fit entirely inside the cheap hours, which matters far more than session size.
